advantages: Tuning complexity explicitly targets the bias–variance sweet spot instead of guessing model size.
Validation curves make underfitting vs overfitting visible as a function of one interpretable knob (depth, degree, k).
Choosing minimal sufficient complexity improves stability, interpretability, and inference cost.
Matching complexity to dataset size avoids wasted compute on models that cannot generalize from available rows.
disadvantages: Too little complexity underfits — high bias caps performance no matter how clean the labels are.
Too much complexity overfits — low training error hides poor validation and production performance.
A single complexity metric (parameter count) misleads when regularization or architecture constraints matter.
Optimal complexity shifts with data volume and noise — a tuned setting does not transfer blindly to new domains.
